7.9 A Pinch of History
Do you know what a fraction was called in ancient India? It twas called bhinna in Sanskrit, which means
'broken'. It t was also called bhaga or ansha meaning 'part' or 'piece'.
The way we write fractions today, globally, originated in India. In ancient Indian mathematical texts, such as the Bakshali manuscript
(from around the year 3oo CE), when they wanted to write
1
they
2'
Wrote it as
12
which is indeed very similar to the way we write it today! This method of writing and working with fractions continued to be used in India for the next several centuries, including by
(499 CE), Brahmagupta
(628 CE), Sridharacharya Aryabhata

question_text:
UMM R
Y
Fraction ase equal share: When a whole number of units is divided into equal parts and shared equally, a fraction results.
Fractional Units: When one e whole basic unit is divided into equal parts, then each part is called a fractional unit.
Reading g Fractions: In a fraction such as
5/6
5 is called the numerator and 6 is called the denominator.
fractions contain a whole number part and a fractional edf I par Number line: Fractions can be shown on a number line. Every fraction has a point associated with it on the number line.
Equivalent t Fractions: When two or more fractions represent the same share or number, they are called equivalent fractions.
Lowest terms: A fraction whose numerator and denominator have no common factor other than 1 is said to be in lowest terms or in its simplest form.
Brahmagupta's method for adding fractions: When adding fractions, convert them i into equivalent f fractions
6 With the same fractional unit
(i.e., the e same denominator), and t then add the number of fractional units in each fraction n to obtain the This s is sum.'
accomplished 1
by adding the e numerators while keeping the same denominator.
Brahmagupta's method for subtracting fractions: When subtracting fractions, them into equivalent fractions With the convert same fractional unit
(i.e., t the denominator), and then subtract the same number of fractional units. This is accomplished by subtracting the numerators while keeping the same denominator.
